walkthrough.title: Unfold the Game Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Lords of Waterdeep set up. First, unfold the game board and place it in the center of the table where everyone can reach it. You will see the city of Waterdeep laid out with nine basic Buildings around the edges, a scoring track along the outside, and a rounds track along the bottom.

walkthrough.title: Set Out the Supply
walkthrough.text: Now let's create the supply. Gather all the Adventurer cubes and place them in a shared pile near the board. There are four types: Clerics are white, Fighters are orange, Rogues are black, and Wizards are purple. Also set out the Gold tokens, both the one-Gold and five-Gold denominations, and the Victory Point tokens. Keep everything within easy reach of all players.

walkthrough.title: Mark the Rounds Track
walkthrough.text: Find the rounds track along the bottom of the board. It has eight numbered spaces, one for each round of the game. Place three Victory Point tokens on each of the eight round spaces. These tokens serve double duty. They mark which round you are on, and at the start of each round they get distributed to Buildings in Builder's Hall.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Builder's Hall
walkthrough.text: Find the stack of twenty-four Building tiles. These are the advanced Buildings that players can purchase during the game. Shuffle them face down and place the stack on the labeled space near Builder's Hall. Then draw three tiles from the top and place them face up in the three spaces of Builder's Hall. These are the Buildings currently available for purchase.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Quest Deck
walkthrough.text: Now let's set up Quests. Find the sixty Quest cards, shuffle them, and deal two face-up Quest cards to each player. These are your starting active Quests. Then place one face-up Quest card in each of the four spaces at Cliffwatch Inn on the board. Place the remaining Quest cards face down in the labeled Quest deck space near Cliffwatch Inn.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Intrigue Deck
walkthrough.text: Find the fifty Intrigue cards. Shuffle them and deal two face down to each player. Keep your Intrigue cards hidden from opponents. These cards let you manipulate the game through attacks, utility effects, and Mandatory Quests. Place the remaining Intrigue cards face down in the labeled space near Waterdeep Harbor.

walkthrough.title: Deal Lord Cards
walkthrough.text: Find the eleven Lord of Waterdeep cards. Shuffle them and deal one face down to each player. This is your secret identity for the entire game. Peek at it but keep it hidden from everyone else. Your Lord card tells you which types of Quests will earn you bonus Victory Points at the end of the game. Return the remaining Lord cards to the box face down without looking at them.

walkthrough.title: Choose Player Colors
walkthrough.text: Each player chooses a color. There are five colors available, each representing a different secret society: Knights of the Shield, Silverstars, City Guard, Harpers, and Red Sashes. Take the player mat that matches your chosen color, along with your matching score marker and Building control markers.

walkthrough.title: Place Score Markers
walkthrough.text: Each player places their circular score marker on the scoring track at the space labeled zero. This track runs along the outside edge of the board. As you score Victory Points during the game, you will advance your marker along this track. There are also one-hundred VP tokens for when your marker laps the track.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Your Agent Pool
walkthrough.text: Now grab your Agents. The number of Agents you start with depends on how many players are in the game. With two players, each person gets four Agents. With three players, you get three each. With four or five players, you get two each. Place your starting Agents on the Agent Pool area of your player mat.

walkthrough.title: Place Your Round Five Agent
walkthrough.text: Each player also has one extra Agent of their color. Place this Agent near the Round five space on the rounds track. This Agent becomes available at the start of Round five, giving every player an additional worker for the rest of the game. It is a nice power boost for the late game.

walkthrough.title: Determine First Player and Starting Gold
walkthrough.text: The player who most recently visited another city goes first. Give that player the First Player marker. The starting player receives four Gold. The next player clockwise gets five Gold, the next gets six, and so on. Each player receives one more Gold than the player to their right, so going later gives you a small Gold bonus to compensate.

summary.title: Choose Player Colors

100 VP tokens available when marker laps the track

summary.title: Set Up Your Agent Pool

Place starting Agents on Agent Pool area of player mat

summary.title: Place Your Round Five Agent
bullets: Place 1 extra Agent near Round 5 space on rounds track

summary.title: Determine First Player and Starting Gold
bullets: Most recent city traveler goes first — give them First Player marker
Starting Gold: 1st player = 4 Gold, 2nd = 5, 3rd = 6, etc.
Each subsequent player gets 1 more Gold than the previous

walkthrough.title: The Eight Rounds
walkthrough.text: Lords of Waterdeep is played over eight rounds. Each round, players take turns clockwise starting from the player with the First Player marker. On your turn, you must assign one Agent from your pool to an action space on the board. When all players have assigned all their Agents, the round ends and Agents return to their pools.

walkthrough.title: Start of Round
walkthrough.text: At the start of each round, remove the three VP tokens from that round's space on the rounds track. Place one VP token on each face-up Building tile in Builder's Hall. This means unsold Buildings become more valuable over time because VP tokens accumulate on them. If any Buildings in play have start-of-round effects, resolve those now too.

walkthrough.title: What You Do on Your Turn
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you do one or both of two things in order. First, you must assign one Agent to an unoccupied action space on any Building. Second, after assigning your Agent, you may complete one Quest if you have the required resources. You cannot skip assigning an Agent if you have one available. If you run out of Agents, you are skipped for the rest of the round.

walkthrough.title: Assigning an Agent
walkthrough.text: To assign an Agent, take one from your Agent Pool and place it on any unoccupied action space on the board. You cannot place an Agent on a space that already has another Agent on it, whether yours or an opponent's. Once placed, you immediately resolve that action space's instructions. Actions typically give you Adventurer cubes, Gold, cards, or other benefits.

walkthrough.title: The Four Adventurer Types
walkthrough.text: The resources you are mainly collecting are Adventurer cubes. There are four types: Clerics are the white cubes, Fighters are orange, Rogues are black, and Wizards are purple. Different Quests require different combinations of these Adventurers plus Gold. When you gain Adventurers from a Building, take them from the general supply and place them in your Tavern area on your player mat.

walkthrough.title: Key Basic Buildings
walkthrough.text: Let's walk through the most important basic Buildings. The Plinth gives you one Cleric. Blackstaff Tower gives you one Wizard. Field of Triumph gives you two Fighters. The Grinning Lion Tavern gives you two Rogues. Aurora's Realms Shop gives you four Gold. Castle Waterdeep lets you take the First Player marker and draw one Intrigue card.

walkthrough.title: Cliffwatch Inn
walkthrough.text: Cliffwatch Inn is where you pick up new Quests. It has three different action spaces, each with a slightly different bonus. The first lets you take one face-up Quest card and two Gold. The second lets you take one Quest card and draw one Intrigue card. The third lets you discard all four face-up Quests, deal four new ones, and then take one. Whenever you take a face-up Quest, immediately replace it from the Quest deck.

walkthrough.title: Builder's Hall
walkthrough.text: Builder's Hall lets you purchase advanced Building tiles. When you assign an Agent here, choose one of the face-up Buildings and pay its Gold cost. You immediately score any VP shown on the tile and place it on an open board space. Put one of your Building control markers on it to show ownership. Then draw a new tile from the stack to refill Builder's Hall. Once a Building is in play, anyone can assign an Agent to it.

walkthrough.title: Building Ownership Benefits
walkthrough.text: Here is why buying Buildings is powerful. When another player assigns their Agent to a Building you own, you gain the owner benefit listed on the tile. This might be a free Adventurer, Gold, VP, or an Intrigue card draw. You gain this benefit automatically, without spending a turn. The more Buildings you own, the more passive income you generate from opponents' actions.

walkthrough.title: Waterdeep Harbor and Intrigue Cards
walkthrough.text: Waterdeep Harbor has three action spaces. When you assign an Agent here, you play one Intrigue card from your hand. Intrigue cards come in three types. Attack cards hurt your opponents. Utility cards help you with extra resources or actions. Mandatory Quest cards force an opponent to complete a minor Quest before they can complete any others. If you have no Intrigue cards in hand, you cannot assign to the Harbor.

walkthrough.title: Harbor Reassignment
walkthrough.text: Waterdeep Harbor has a special end-of-round bonus. After all Agents have been assigned for the round, each player who has an Agent in the Harbor gets to reassign it to any other unoccupied action space. This is like getting an extra turn. Reassignment happens in order, starting from the Harbor space numbered one. You cannot reassign back to the Harbor. After reassigning, you may also complete a Quest.

walkthrough.title: Completing a Quest
walkthrough.text: After assigning or reassigning an Agent, you may complete one Quest. Check the requirements on your Quest card. It will list specific Adventurer types and sometimes Gold that you must pay. Remove those resources from your Tavern and return them to the supply. Then collect the reward shown on the card, which usually includes VP and sometimes extra resources. Place the completed Quest face down in your Completed Quests area.

walkthrough.title: Quest Types and Your Lord
walkthrough.text: There are five Quest types: Arcana, Commerce, Piety, Skullduggery, and Warfare. Your secret Lord card grants bonus VP at the end of the game for completing Quests of specific types. For example, a Lord might grant four bonus VP for each Arcana and Warfare Quest you complete. This means you want to focus on Quests that match your Lord, but be careful not to make your identity too obvious to opponents.

walkthrough.title: Plot Quests
walkthrough.text: Some Quests are marked as Plot Quests, identifiable by a differently colored name on the card. When you complete a Plot Quest, you earn the normal reward, but you also gain an ongoing benefit that lasts for the rest of the game. For example, Recover the Magister's Orb lets you assign an Agent to an occupied space once per round. Place completed Plot Quests face up near your mat as a reminder.

walkthrough.title: Mandatory Quests
walkthrough.text: Mandatory Quests are a type of Intrigue card that you play on an opponent. When hit with a Mandatory Quest, that player must complete it before they can complete any other Quest. Mandatory Quests have small requirements but offer minimal rewards. They are a tempo hit designed to slow someone down. If a Mandatory Quest is not completed by the end of the game, it costs four VP as a penalty.

walkthrough.title: The Round Five Power Spike
walkthrough.text: At the start of Round five, each player adds the extra Agent they placed near the rounds track to their pool. This gives everyone an additional action each turn for the remaining four rounds. The game accelerates at this point because more Agents are on the board. Plan ahead so you have high-value Quests or Building plays ready to take advantage of this boost.

walkthrough.title: End of Round
walkthrough.text: When all Agents have been assigned and any Harbor reassignments are done, the round ends. All players return their Agents to their pools. The player with the First Player marker begins the next round. The game continues for eight rounds total.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: After Round eight, the game ends and final scoring begins. First, keep all VP you already earned on the scoring track. Then every player reveals their Lord card and scores their Lord bonus VP. Next, each leftover Adventurer in your Tavern is worth one VP, and every two Gold in your Tavern is worth one VP, rounding down. Finally, subtract four VP for each incomplete Mandatory Quest. The player with the most VP wins. If there is a tie, the player with the most Gold wins.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Waterdeep
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Lords of Waterdeep! In this practice tutorial, we will walk through the first two rounds with four example players. Each player will demonstrate different strategies and common pitfalls so you can see how the game flows. Follow along even if you have fewer players at your table.

walkthrough.title: Meet the Players
walkthrough.text: Player 1 has the First Player marker and four Gold. Player 2 has five Gold. Player 3 has six Gold. Player 4 has seven Gold. Each player has received two Quest cards, two Intrigue cards, and one secret Lord card. Let's peek at the Lords: Player 1 scores bonus VP for Warfare and Piety Quests, while Player 3 scores bonus VP for Arcana and Commerce Quests.

walkthrough.title: Start of Round One
walkthrough.text: Before anyone takes a turn, we handle the start of round. Remove the three VP tokens from the Round one space on the rounds track. Place one VP token on each of the three face-up Buildings in Builder's Hall. Those Buildings are now worth a little extra VP if someone purchases them this round.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Grab Fighters
walkthrough.text: Player 1 needs Fighters for a Warfare Quest. They place an Agent on Field of Triumph and take two orange Fighter cubes from the supply, placing them in their Tavern. Simple and effective. Player 1 now has the Adventurers to start working toward their Quest requirements.

walkthrough.title: Player 2: Pick Up a Quest
walkthrough.text: Player 2 assigns an Agent to Cliffwatch Inn and takes one of the face-up Quest cards plus two Gold. A new Quest card immediately replaces the one taken. Player 2 now has three active Quests to choose from, giving them more flexibility in deciding which resources to pursue.

walkthrough.title: Player 3: The Mistake — Buying a Building Too Early
walkthrough.text: Player 3 has six Gold and sees a Building in Builder's Hall that costs four Gold. It looks great, so they assign an Agent to Builder's Hall and buy it. They score a VP or two and place the tile on the board. But now Player 3 has only two Gold left, no Adventurers, and has used one of their precious early-round actions on a Building instead of gathering resources.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Don't Buy Buildings Before You Have Resources
walkthrough.text: Buildings are powerful for their owner benefits, but only over many rounds. In the early game, you need Adventurers to complete Quests and score VP. A Building purchased in Round one generates owner benefits only if other players use it. If nobody assigns to your Building for two rounds, you paid Gold and spent a turn for almost nothing. Buy Buildings in Rounds two through four when you have a resource cushion.

walkthrough.title: Player 4: Waterdeep Harbor Play
walkthrough.text: Player 4 assigns an Agent to Waterdeep Harbor and plays a Utility Intrigue card, gaining two Adventurers of any type. Nice. But the real power is that at the end of the round, Player 4 will reassign this Agent to another action space, essentially getting a bonus turn. Waterdeep Harbor is one of the strongest plays in the game because of this double action.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Complete a Quest
walkthrough.text: Player 1 assigns their second Agent to The Grinning Lion Tavern and takes two Rogues. Now they have two Fighters and two Rogues. After assigning, Player 1 checks their Quests and finds one that requires exactly two Fighters and two Rogues. They return those cubes to the supply and score the VP reward. Their first Quest is done!

walkthrough.title: Why Completing Quests Early Matters
walkthrough.text: Player 1 completed a Quest on their second turn of the game. This is great because VP on the track is guaranteed. Adventurers sitting in your Tavern score nothing during the game. Convert them to Quest rewards as quickly as possible. The earlier you start scoring, the more momentum you build.

walkthrough.title: Player 2: The Mistake — Hoarding Cubes
walkthrough.text: Player 2 assigns to The Plinth and takes one Cleric. They now have a growing collection of cubes in their Tavern but have not completed a single Quest. They are holding resources that could be scoring points. Meanwhile, Player 1 has already scored VP and freed up their Tavern for the next round.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Convert Resources to VP Promptly
walkthrough.text: Stockpiling Adventurers without completing Quests is one of the most common beginner mistakes. Every cube in your Tavern represents potential points sitting idle. Look at your Quest requirements before taking actions. Grab the specific resources you need and complete the Quest as soon as possible. Targeted collecting beats random hoarding every time.

walkthrough.title: End of Round One: Harbor Reassignment
walkthrough.text: All Agents are placed. Now Player 4 gets to reassign their Agent from Waterdeep Harbor. They move it to Blackstaff Tower and take one Wizard from the supply. Player 4 may also complete a Quest after reassigning. This is the Harbor payoff. One Agent placement gave Player 4 an Intrigue effect plus a full action at another Building.

walkthrough.title: Agents Return to Pools
walkthrough.text: Round one is over. All players pick up their Agents and return them to their pools. The First Player marker stays with Player 1 unless someone visited Castle Waterdeep to claim it. Everyone reviews their Quests and plans for Round two.

walkthrough.title: Start of Round Two
walkthrough.text: Remove the three VP tokens from the Round two space. Place one VP token on each face-up Building in Builder's Hall. If a Building already had a VP token from Round one, it now has two. Buildings that sit unpurchased become increasingly valuable. Now is a better time for Player 3 to consider buying one.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Castle Waterdeep
walkthrough.text: Player 1 assigns to Castle Waterdeep, taking the First Player marker and drawing one Intrigue card. Now Player 1 will go first in Round three and has an Intrigue card to play at the Harbor later. Controlling turn order is a subtle but powerful advantage, especially when competing for a contested action space.

walkthrough.title: Player 3: The Mistake — Revealing Your Lord
walkthrough.text: Player 3's Lord scores bonus VP for Arcana and Commerce Quests. Player 3 completes their second Arcana Quest and immediately picks up a third Arcana Quest from Cliffwatch Inn. The pattern is obvious. Opponents now know Player 3's Lord and can block Arcana-related Buildings and Quests. Player 4 plays a Mandatory Quest on Player 3 to slow them down further.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Disguise Your Lord's Identity
walkthrough.text: Your Lord card is secret for a reason. If opponents figure out which Quest types earn you bonus VP, they can deny you key resources, block Buildings you need, or saddle you with Mandatory Quests. Mix in a Quest from a non-bonus type every few rounds to keep them guessing. A couple of off-type Quests early on is a small sacrifice for long-term secrecy.

walkthrough.title: Player 4: Plot Quest Completion
walkthrough.text: Player 4 completes a Plot Quest this round, earning VP and gaining an ongoing effect. For example, Recruit Lieutenant adds the Lieutenant piece to their Agent pool permanently. This extra Agent gives Player 4 one more placement every round for the rest of the game. Plot Quests are incredibly powerful if completed early.

walkthrough.title: Continue Playing
walkthrough.text: The game continues in this rhythm for eight rounds. Assign Agents, collect resources, complete Quests, and use Intrigue cards to gain advantages or hinder opponents. Remember that Round five adds an extra Agent to each player's pool. The game accelerates from that point, so have your strategy ready.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations! You have seen the core flow of Lords of Waterdeep. You have watched players collect Adventurers, complete Quests, buy Buildings, play Intrigue cards, and exploit the Harbor for bonus actions. You have also seen three critical mistakes: buying Buildings before building resources, hoarding cubes without completing Quests, and revealing your Lord's identity too early. Avoid those traps and you will have a strong first game.

walkthrough.title: Before You Begin
walkthrough.text:
Before you start a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics and beginner advice.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to Buildings, Quest types, and key terms.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

summary.title: Before You Begin
bullets: Check out General Tips → strategy basics for beginners
Reference the Appendix → quick guide to Buildings, Quests, terms
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before diving into strategy, here are the biggest mistakes new players make. First, hoarding Adventurers without completing Quests, which leaves points sitting idle in your Tavern. Second, buying expensive Buildings too early when you should be collecting resources. Third, telegraphing your Lord by pursuing only one Quest type, which invites targeted blocking. Fourth, ignoring Waterdeep Harbor and missing out on the powerful double-action it provides. Keep these in mind and your first game will be much stronger.

walkthrough.title: Quest Selection Strategy
walkthrough.text: When picking Quests from Cliffwatch Inn, think about efficiency over raw VP value. The best Quests overlap in resource requirements with Quests you already have. If two of your active Quests both need Fighters, you can focus your actions on Fighter-producing Buildings and complete both Quests in quick succession. Avoid holding more than three or four active Quests at once. Too many Quests scatter your focus and slow you down.

walkthrough.title: The Power of Waterdeep Harbor
walkthrough.text: Waterdeep Harbor is arguably the strongest location on the board. By assigning there, you play an Intrigue card for its immediate effect, and then at the end of the round you reassign your Agent to any other open space. That is two actions for the price of one. Try to visit the Harbor at least once every round if you have Intrigue cards. Stock up on Intrigue cards from Castle Waterdeep to keep this option available.

walkthrough.title: When to Buy Buildings
walkthrough.text: The ideal time to buy a Building is Rounds two through four. By then, you have a resource base, and the Building still has many rounds to generate owner benefits. When choosing which Building to buy, consider two things. First, how useful is the action space? If it produces resources that many players need, opponents will use it frequently, triggering your owner benefit often. Second, does the action synergize with your own strategy? A Building you want to visit yourself plus one that gives you income when others visit is the dream.

walkthrough.title: Managing Your Lord's Identity
walkthrough.text: Your Lord card is worth a significant chunk of your final score, often twenty to thirty VP. Protecting your identity prevents opponents from blocking your preferred Quest types or targeting you with Mandatory Quests. In the first three rounds, complete at least one Quest outside your bonus types to throw opponents off. By Round five, opponents will likely suspect your Lord, but by then you should have enough momentum that targeted blocking matters less.

walkthrough.title: Timing Quests Around the Extra Agent
walkthrough.text: Round five is a turning point because every player gains an extra Agent. Plan ahead so you enter Round five with at least one high-value Quest ready to complete. The extra Agent gives you more flexibility to grab resources and complete Quests in the same round. Players who are already set up to exploit the extra Agent often pull ahead at this critical juncture.

walkthrough.title: Mandatory Quest Defense
walkthrough.text: Mandatory Quests are annoying but manageable if you plan for them. Keep a small reserve of common Adventurers in your Tavern so you can complete a Mandatory Quest quickly without derailing your entire round. If you are leading or your Lord identity is known, expect to get hit. The four VP penalty for an incomplete Mandatory Quest at game end is steep. Do not leave one sitting uncompleted.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Scoring Awareness
walkthrough.text: In the final rounds, every decision should maximize VP. Remember that leftover Adventurers are worth one VP each and every two Gold is worth one VP. If you cannot complete another Quest, gathering resources is still worth a few points. Also double-check your Lord card scoring. Count how many bonus Quests you have completed and see if squeezing in one more matching Quest is feasible. Those four bonus VP per Quest add up fast.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't hoard cubes — idle Adventurers score nothing mid-game
Don't buy expensive Buildings in Round 1 — gather resources first
Don't telegraph your Lord by chasing only one Quest type
Don't ignore Waterdeep Harbor — it's the strongest double-action play

summary.title: Quest Selection Strategy
bullets: Pick Quests with overlapping resource requirements
Focus on 2-3 active Quests — too many scatter your focus
Prioritize VP-efficient Quests that match your Lord

summary.title: The Power of Waterdeep Harbor
bullets: Play Intrigue + reassign Agent = 2 actions for 1 placement
Visit Harbor at least once per round if you have Intrigue cards
Stock up on Intrigue cards via Castle Waterdeep

summary.title: When to Buy Buildings
bullets: Ideal purchase window: Rounds 2-4
Best Buildings: popular action space + strong owner benefit
VP tokens on tiles grow each round — delayed buys yield bonus VP

summary.title: Managing Your Lord's Identity
bullets: Lord bonus often worth 20-30 VP — protect your identity
Complete off-type Quests early to camouflage
By Round 5, secrecy matters less — momentum carries you

summary.title: Timing Quests Around the Extra Agent
bullets: Round 5 = extra Agent = power spike for all players
Enter Round 5 with high-value Quests ready to complete
Extra Agent = more flexibility to collect and complete in same round

summary.title: Mandatory Quest Defense
bullets: Keep a small cube reserve to clear Mandatory Quests quickly
Expect Mandatory Quests when leading or Lord is exposed
-4 VP penalty at game end if incomplete — never leave one hanging

summary.title: Endgame Scoring Awareness
bullets: Leftover Adventurers = 1 VP each; 2 Gold = 1 VP
Last-round resource grabs still score if no Quest available
Count Lord bonus — one more matching Quest = +4 VP

walkthrough.title: Quest Chaining Strategy
walkthrough.text: The most efficient players chain Quests that share resource requirements. If three of your active Quests all need Fighters, visiting Field of Triumph and Fighter-producing Buildings serves all of them simultaneously. The goal is to minimize wasted actions. Every Agent placement should advance at least one Quest, and ideally set up for the next one. Sequence your Quests so that completing one frees up resources or generates rewards that feed directly into the next.

walkthrough.title: Plot Quest Priority
walkthrough.text: Plot Quests should be your first priority in the early game. Their ongoing effects compound over the remaining rounds. Recruit Lieutenant adds a permanent extra Agent, which is essentially gaining the Round five bonus early. Recover the Magister's Orb lets you assign to occupied spaces, breaking the fundamental constraint of the game. Complete Plot Quests in Rounds one through three for maximum value. By Rounds six through eight, the ongoing effect has too few rounds left to justify the investment.

walkthrough.title: Building Economy Mastery
walkthrough.text: Advanced players view Building purchases as investments. Calculate the breakeven point: if a Building costs four Gold and its owner benefit is one Rogue, it pays for itself after opponents assign there four times. In a four-player game, popular Buildings can trigger two or three times per round, meaning you break even in two rounds and generate pure profit after that. The best Buildings to purchase have action spaces that produce resources multiple players need.

walkthrough.title: Harbor Tempo Advantage
walkthrough.text: Expert players use the Harbor strategically. Because Harbor Agents reassign after all other placements, you get to see the entire board state before choosing your action. This information advantage lets you snipe the last copy of a contested resource or take the optimal remaining space. Combine this with Attack Intrigue cards to disrupt the leader while gaining a free action. Two or even three Harbor visits in a single round is not unusual for top players.

walkthrough.title: Denial and Blocking
walkthrough.text: In the mid to late game, blocking opponents is as valuable as advancing your own position. If you know an opponent needs Wizards to complete a high-value Arcana Quest, placing your Agent on Blackstaff Tower denies them that resource even if you do not need Wizards yourself. This is especially powerful with few players, where blocking is more impactful. Combine blocking with Mandatory Quests to create a double tempo hit on the leader.

walkthrough.title: Lord-Based Meta Strategy
walkthrough.text: Different Lord cards support different strategic emphases. Lords that reward two Quest types are the most common and encourage a focused but diversified approach. Some Lords reward Buildings or Intrigue plays, creating entirely different incentive structures. When your Lord rewards Building ownership, buy early and often. When your Lord rewards specific Quest types, stockpile those Quests and complete them in bursts to maximize efficiency.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Sprint Tactics
walkthrough.text: Rounds seven and eight are where games are won and lost. At this point, stop investing in engine building and focus entirely on maximizing VP per Agent placement. Complete your highest-value Quests first. If you have leftover Gold, consider a final Building purchase for the immediate VP on the tile plus accumulated VP tokens. Any Adventurers you cannot convert to Quests are still worth one VP each. Every action should add directly to your score.

walkthrough.title: Reading Opponents and Adapting
walkthrough.text: Watch what Quests your opponents are collecting and which Buildings they visit repeatedly. If someone keeps taking Clerics and completing Piety Quests, their Lord likely rewards Piety. Use this information to time your Mandatory Quests for maximum disruption and to contest their key resources. Flexibility is essential. The best players maintain two or three viable Quests at all times so they can adapt when opponents block their primary plan.

summary.title: Quest Chaining Strategy
bullets: Pick Quests with overlapping resource needs (e.g., all need Fighters)
Every Agent placement should advance at least one Quest
Sequence completions so one Quest's reward feeds the next

summary.title: Plot Quest Priority
bullets: Complete Plot Quests in Rounds 1-3 for max compound value
Recruit Lieutenant = permanent extra Agent
Recover the Magister's Orb = assign to occupied spaces
After Round 5, ongoing effects have too few rounds to matter

summary.title: Building Economy Mastery
bullets: Calculate breakeven: cost vs. frequency of owner benefit triggers
Popular Buildings can trigger 2-3 times/round in 4-5 player games
Best buys: action space that many players need

summary.title: Harbor Tempo Advantage
bullets: Harbor Agents reassign last → full board information before choosing
Snipe contested resources or optimal remaining spaces
2-3 Harbor visits per round = strong tempo engine

summary.title: Denial and Blocking
bullets: Block opponents' key resources, especially in late game
Combine blocking with Mandatory Quests for double tempo hit
More impactful with fewer players (2-3p)

summary.title: Lord-Based Meta Strategy
bullets: 2-type Lords: focus Quest selection, diversify to camouflage
Building-reward Lords: buy early and often
Quest-type Lords: stockpile and burst-complete matching Quests

summary.title: Endgame Sprint Tactics
bullets: Rounds 7-8: stop engine-building, maximize VP per action
Complete highest-value Quests first
Final Building purchase = immediate VP + accumulated tokens
Unconverted Adventurers = 1 VP each; 2 Gold = 1 VP

summary.title: Reading Opponents and Adapting
bullets: Track opponents' Quest types and Building visits to deduce their Lord
Time Mandatory Quests for maximum disruption on leader
Maintain 2-3 viable Quests to adapt when blocked

entries.definition: A wooden piece placed on action spaces to take actions. Each player has a pool of Agents determined by player count. You assign one Agent per turn and resolve the action immediately.
The area on your player mat where unassigned Agents wait. At the end of each round, all assigned Agents return here. You must assign an Agent if you have one available.
The area on your player mat where you store Adventurer cubes and Gold until you spend them to complete Quests. Resources in your Tavern are visible to all players.
A specific spot on a Building where one Agent can be placed. Each action space provides a defined benefit when an Agent is assigned to it. Only one Agent can occupy a space at a time.
Wooden cubes representing hired heroes. Four types exist: Clerics (white), Fighters (orange), Rogues (black), and Wizards (purple). Spent from your Tavern to complete Quests.
White Adventurer cube. Primarily needed for Piety Quests. Obtained from The Plinth (basic Building) and several advanced Buildings.
Orange Adventurer cube. Primarily needed for Warfare Quests. Obtained from Field of Triumph (basic Building), which gives two Fighters per visit.
Black Adventurer cube. Primarily needed for Skullduggery Quests. Obtained from The Grinning Lion Tavern (basic Building), which gives two Rogues per visit.
Purple Adventurer cube. Primarily needed for Arcana Quests. Obtained from Blackstaff Tower (basic Building), which gives only one Wizard per visit, making Wizards the scarcest resource.
Currency used to purchase Buildings, complete certain Quests, and pay for game effects. Comes in one-Gold and five-Gold denominations. At game end, every two Gold is worth one VP.
The primary scoring currency. Tracked on the scoring track around the board edge. Earned from completing Quests, purchasing Buildings, and various game effects. Highest VP after final scoring wins.
A card representing a task to complete. Lists required Adventurers and Gold on the Requires line and the reward (usually VP) on the Reward line. Active Quests are face up in front of you.
One of five Quest types. Arcana Quests typically require Wizards and reward VP. Associated with magical and scholarly pursuits in Waterdeep.
One of five Quest types. Commerce Quests typically require a mix of Adventurer types plus Gold. Associated with trade and mercantile dealings.
One of five Quest types. Piety Quests typically require Clerics. Associated with religious and charitable endeavors in Waterdeep.
One of five Quest types. Skullduggery Quests typically require Rogues. Associated with espionage, theft, and subterfuge.
One of five Quest types. Warfare Quests typically require Fighters. Associated with military and combat operations.
A special Quest with a differently colored name. Completing it grants the normal reward plus an ongoing effect for the rest of the game. Keep completed Plot Quests face up near your mat.

An Intrigue card type played on an opponent. The targeted player must complete this minor Quest before completing any others. Costs -4 VP at game end if incomplete. Completed Mandatory Quests go to the Intrigue discard pile.

A card played by assigning an Agent to Waterdeep Harbor. Three types: Attack (hurts opponents), Utility (helps you), and Mandatory Quest (delays an opponent). Drawn from Castle Waterdeep and some Buildings.
A type of Intrigue card that penalizes opponents, often removing their Adventurers or forcing them to give up resources. May also grant a small benefit to the player who plays it.
A type of Intrigue card that provides a benefit to the player who plays it, such as gaining Adventurers, Gold, or other resources. Does not directly harm opponents.
A secret identity card dealt at setup. Grants bonus VP at game end for specific accomplishments, usually completing Quests of two particular types. Worth roughly 20-30 VP in a typical game.

One of nine Buildings printed on the game board. Always available for Agent assignment. Includes The Plinth, Blackstaff Tower, Field of Triumph, The Grinning Lion Tavern, Aurora's Realms Shop, Castle Waterdeep, Cliffwatch Inn, Builder's Hall, and Waterdeep Harbor.
A Building tile purchased from Builder's Hall. Placed on an open board space with the buyer's control marker. Provides an action space for all players and an owner benefit when non-owners use it.

A bonus the Building owner receives whenever another player assigns an Agent to their Building. Benefits include free Adventurers, Gold, VP, or Intrigue card draws. Triggers automatically without the owner spending a turn.
Basic Building where players purchase advanced Building tiles. At start of each round, each face-up tile gains one VP token. When a tile is purchased, a new one is drawn from the stack.
Basic Building with three action spaces for acquiring Quest cards. Space 1: Quest + 2 Gold. Space 2: Quest + 1 Intrigue card. Space 3: Discard all face-up Quests, deal 4 new, take 1.

Basic Building with three action spaces. Assigning here requires playing one Intrigue card. After all Agents are assigned, Harbor Agents are reassigned to other open action spaces in numbered order, granting bonus turns.
Basic Building. Action: take the First Player marker and draw one Intrigue card. Controls turn order for the next round and provides Intrigue fuel for Harbor plays.
A die-cut token matching a player's color, placed on purchased Buildings to indicate ownership. Each player has nine markers available.
A neutral Agent piece that starts uncontrolled. Gained by assigning to the Palace of Waterdeep Building. Functions as an additional Agent for the player who controls it.
A neutral Agent piece that starts uncontrolled. Gained by completing the Recruit Lieutenant Plot Quest. Permanently adds to the player's Agent pool for the rest of the game.
A token held by the player who goes first each round. Can be claimed by assigning to Castle Waterdeep. The starting player goes first, then play proceeds clockwise.
An eight-space track on the board marking game progression. Starts with three VP tokens on each space. Tokens are removed at start of each round and distributed to Builder's Hall Buildings.
The track running around the outside edge of the board where score markers are moved as players earn VP. When a marker passes zero, take a 100 VP token.

Occurs after Round 8. Reveal Lord cards and score their bonus VP. Each leftover Adventurer = 1 VP. Every 2 Gold = 1 VP (rounded down). Subtract 4 VP per incomplete Mandatory Quest. Highest VP wins; tiebreaker is most Gold.
At the end of each round, Agents in Waterdeep Harbor are moved to other unoccupied action spaces. This grants an effective bonus turn. Reassignment happens in Harbor space numbered order (1, 2, 3).
